seo.title: Krabi Province, Thailand — Complete Guide 2026
seo.metaDescription: Complete guide to Krabi Province: Railay Beach, Phi Phi Islands, Tiger Cave Temple, and 150+ islands. Area: 4,709 km², 8 districts. Plan your trip.

overview: Krabi is a coastal province on the Andaman Sea in southern Thailand, renowned for its dramatic limestone karst formations, pristine white-sand beaches, and over 150 islands including the world-famous Phi Phi archipelago. Despite being one of Thailand's top beach destinations, much of the province remains covered in lush rainforest and mangrove ecosystems. The provincial capital, Krabi Town, sits along the Krabi River estuary and serves as a gateway to Railay Beach, Ao Nang, Ko Lanta, and the Four Islands.
geography: Krabi Province covers 4,709 km² along the Andaman Sea coast, bordered by dramatic limestone karst mountains that define the landscape. The coastline stretches approximately 160 km and includes sheltered bays, mangrove forests, and hundreds of islands. The interior is mountainous and densely forested, with Khao Phanom Bencha (1,397 m) as the highest peak. The province features unique geological formations including the Shell Fossil Beach (Susan Hoi), containing 75-million-year-old fossilized shells. Major waterways include the Krabi River, which flows through the capital into the Andaman Sea.
highlights: Railay Beach — stunning peninsula accessible only by boat, surrounded by towering limestone cliffs and world-class rock climbing
Phi Phi Islands — iconic limestone archipelago with crystal-clear waters, snorkeling, and Maya Bay (featured in The Beach)
Tiger Cave Temple (Wat Tham Suea) — 1,237 steps to a hilltop golden Buddha with panoramic views over Krabi
Emerald Pool (Sa Morakot) — natural freshwater pool with vivid emerald-green water in Thung Teao Forest
Hong Islands — protected national park with stunning lagoons, sea caves, and pristine snorkeling spots
Ao Nang — Krabi's main beach resort area and departure point for island-hopping tours
Ko Lanta — laid-back island district with long beaches, diving, and Old Town fishing village charm
Shell Fossil Beach (Susan Hoi) — 75-million-year-old fossilized shell deposits along the coastline

bestTimeToVisit.peak: Nov–Feb: Dry northeast monsoon season with temperatures of 25–32°C, calm Andaman seas, and clear skies. Ideal for beach activities, island hopping, diving, and rock climbing at Railay. Highest hotel prices and tourist crowds, especially around Christmas and New Year.
bestTimeToVisit.shoulder: Mar–May: Hot season with temperatures reaching 33–36°C and high humidity. Seas remain mostly calm through April, making island trips still viable. Songkran (mid-April) brings festive celebrations. Hotel prices begin to drop.
bestTimeToVisit.lowSeason: Jun–Oct: Southwest monsoon brings regular rain and rougher seas. Some island tours (especially Hong Islands and Phi Phi) may be suspended during storms. Hotel prices drop 30–50%. Ko Lanta's east coast and sheltered Ao Nang areas remain accessible. September–October is wettest.
bestTimeToVisit.recommendation: Visit November to February for the best weather, calm seas, and optimal diving visibility. Budget travelers should consider June or early July — afternoon showers are manageable and prices are significantly lower. Avoid booking island tours during heavy monsoon storms in September–October.

topAttractions.name: Railay Beach
topAttractions.description: A stunning peninsula surrounded by sheer limestone cliffs, accessible only by longtail boat from Ao Nang or Krabi Town. Railay features four beaches (Railay West, Railay East, Phra Nang, and Tonsai), a viewpoint lagoon, and the famous Phra Nang Cave shrine. The area is world-renowned for rock climbing with over 700 bolted routes on the karst walls.
topAttractions.entranceFee: Free (longtail boat from Ao Nang: 100 THB one-way)

topAttractions.name: Phi Phi Islands (Hat Noppharat Thara–Mu Ko Phi Phi National Park)
topAttractions.description: An archipelago of six islands featuring towering limestone cliffs, turquoise lagoons, and vibrant coral reefs. Ko Phi Phi Don is the main inhabited island with accommodation and nightlife. Ko Phi Phi Leh houses the famous Maya Bay, reopened with daily visitor caps to protect the ecosystem. Snorkeling, diving, and boat tours are the main activities.

topAttractions.name: Tiger Cave Temple (Wat Tham Suea)
topAttractions.description: One of Krabi's most iconic landmarks, this Buddhist temple complex is set into limestone caves and surrounded by rainforest. The main attraction is the 1,237-step staircase leading to a hilltop golden Buddha statue with sweeping 360-degree views over Krabi Town, the coastline, and karst mountains. The cave temple at the base contains tiger-paw-shaped formations that gave the temple its name.

topAttractions.name: Emerald Pool (Sa Morakot) & Hot Springs
topAttractions.description: A natural freshwater pool in the Thung Teao Forest Natural Park with vivid emerald-green water caused by mineral deposits. The pool is fed by a warm stream flowing through lowland rainforest. Nearby, the Klong Thom Hot Springs feature natural hot spring pools set in a forested area, ideal for a relaxing soak. Both attractions are in Khlong Thom district.
topAttractions.entranceFee: Emerald Pool: 200 THB (foreigners); Hot Springs: 90 THB; combined: 290 THB

topAttractions.name: Hong Islands (Mu Ko Hong National Park)
topAttractions.description: A protected national park comprising a cluster of small islands with hidden lagoons, sea caves, and coral reefs. Hong Island's centerpiece is a dramatic enclosed lagoon surrounded by vertical limestone walls, accessible by kayak at certain tides. The marine park offers excellent snorkeling among diverse coral formations and tropical fish.

topAttractions.name: Four Islands (Koh Poda, Chicken Island, Tup Island, Phra Nang Cave Beach)
topAttractions.description: Krabi's most popular day-trip route visits four stunning locations in the Andaman Sea. Koh Poda features a long white-sand beach, Chicken Island (Koh Kai) is named for its chicken-head-shaped rock, and Tup Island reveals a sand spit connecting two islands at low tide. The trip typically includes snorkeling stops and lunch on the beach.
topAttractions.entranceFee: 400 THB national park fee (foreigners); day trip tours from 800–1,500 THB

topAttractions.name: Ko Lanta Old Town
topAttractions.description: A charming historic fishing village on the east coast of Ko Lanta Yai with stilted wooden houses, Chinese shophouses, and a relaxed multicultural atmosphere. The town reflects the island's heritage as a trading post with Chinese, Malay, and Chao Ley (sea gypsy) communities. Seafood restaurants line the waterfront and the area offers a peaceful contrast to the west coast tourist beaches.

topAttractions.name: Shell Fossil Beach (Susan Hoi)
topAttractions.description: A unique geological site containing 75-million-year-old fossilized freshwater snail shells compressed into flat limestone slabs along the shoreline. It is one of only three such fossil shell beaches in the world (the others are in Japan and the United States). The site includes a small visitor center with interpretive displays about the fossils' formation.

localFood: Kua Kling — dry-fried curry with minced pork or beef, shrimp paste, and intense southern Thai spices; Krabi's signature dish and far spicier than central Thai curries
Tom Som Pla — sour and spicy fish soup with tamarind, palm sugar, and smoked fish; a staple of southern Thai coastal cooking
Choo Chee Pla Kapong — whole sea bass in rich red curry coconut sauce with sweet basil and kaffir lime leaves
Crab Curry with Betel Leaves — fresh local crab simmered in coconut milk with bai cha ploo (wild betel leaves) adding a peppery, numbing sensation
Chakteen (Dog Conch) — steamed large sea snails served with dipping sauce, a coastal delicacy found at Krabi night markets
Roti Krabi-style — flaky pan-fried flatbread served with condensed milk, banana, or savory curries; influenced by the province's Muslim Malay heritage
Khao Mok Gai — southern Thai chicken biryani with yellow rice, fried shallots, and cucumber relish, reflecting Krabi's Muslim culinary traditions
culture: Krabi's culture is shaped by its diverse population of Thai Buddhists, Thai-Malay Muslims, Chinese descendants, and the indigenous Chao Ley (sea gypsy) communities on Ko Lanta and other islands. About 40% of the province's population is Muslim, giving Krabi a distinctive blend of Buddhist temples, mosques, and halal food culture. The Chao Ley Loy Rua (floating boat) festival on Ko Lanta, held annually in November, celebrates the sea gypsy heritage with traditional boat ceremonies to honor ancestral spirits. The province's interior retains a rural Thai character with rubber and palm oil plantations as the primary agricultural industries outside of tourism.
gettingThere: Krabi International Airport (KBV) receives direct flights from Bangkok (1h 20min, from 1,000–3,500 THB one-way with AirAsia, Thai Lion Air, or Nok Air). International flights connect from Singapore, Kuala Lumpur, and seasonal charter destinations. VIP buses from Bangkok's Southern Bus Terminal take 10–12 hours (700–1,200 THB). Minivans from Phuket (2.5–3h, 200–350 THB), Surat Thani (3h, 200–300 THB), and Hat Yai (4h, 300–400 THB) are widely available. Ferries connect Ko Lanta and Ko Phi Phi to Phuket and other Andaman coast islands.
gettingAround: Songthaew (shared pickup trucks) run between Krabi Town, Ao Nang, and the airport for 60–150 THB. Longtail boats from Ao Nang to Railay Beach cost 100 THB per person (200 THB return). Grab is available in Krabi Town and Ao Nang for car rides (50–400 THB). Motorbike rental is popular (200–300 THB/day, international license required). Ko Lanta is best explored by motorbike. For island hopping, longtail boats and speedboat tours depart daily from Ao Nang, Nopparat Thara Pier, and Krabi Town's passenger pier.
travelTips: Longtail boats to Railay stop running at 6 PM in low season — plan your return accordingly or stay overnight
Maya Bay on Phi Phi Leh has daily visitor caps — book morning tours for the best chance of entry
Krabi's night markets (Chao Fah Pier on Friday, Saturday, and Sunday) are the best place for cheap local food from 30 THB
Tiger Cave Temple's 1,237 steps are steep and exposed to sun — go early morning and bring at least 1 liter of water
Monsoon season (Jun–Oct) makes some island tours dangerous — always check sea conditions before booking
Ko Lanta is a separate island requiring a car ferry — allow 2+ hours from Krabi Town including the ferry wait
ATMs charge 220 THB per withdrawal — bring a travel card with no foreign transaction fees to save money
Rock climbing at Railay is world-class — even beginners can join half-day courses (from 1,000 THB) with certified guides
